sentences.base_language: Sara al-Najjar is a student who is about to graduate and is looking for work.
sentences.target_language: سارة النجار طالبةٌ وستتخرَّج قريباً، لذا تبحث عن عمل.
sentences.romanized: Sara an-Najjar talibatun wa-sa-tatakharraju qariban, lidha tabhathu 'an 'amal.
sentences.grammarNote:
**طالبةٌ** (taliba) = female student — **ة** (ta' marbuta) is the most common feminine marker in Arabic nouns: طالب (male student) → طالب**ة** (female student), مبرمج (male programmer) → مبرمج**ة** (female programmer). The ة is pronounced when adding a vowel or suffix, silent in a pause.
**ستتخرَّج** = she will graduate — **سـ** (sa-) prefixed to the present tense marks the future: **س**أذهب (I will go), **س**يعود (he will return). The verb تخرَّج is Form V (تَفعَّل) of خرج: تخرَّج = graduated (a specialised meaning of 'went out'). **لذا** = therefore — a formal connector (more formal than **لذلك**).

sentences.base_language: Today she has an interview for an engineer position and feels very nervous.
sentences.target_language: اليوم لديها مقابلةٌ لوظيفة مهندسة وتشعر بتوتر شديد.
sentences.romanized: Al-yawm ladayha muqabalatun li-wazifat muhandisa wa-tash'ur bi-tawattur shadid.
sentences.grammarNote:
**لديها** = she has, lit. 'at her' — **لدى** + ها: Arabic expresses possession with **عند** or **لدى** (both meaning 'at/with'): لديّ كتاب (I have a book, lit. 'at me a book'), لديه وقت (he has time). No separate verb 'to have' exists in Arabic for possession in the present tense.
**مهندسة** = female engineer — مهندس (masc.) + ة (fem. marker). **وظيفة** (wazifa) = position/job (from وظَّف, to appoint). **تشعر بـ** = feels — **شعر بـ** (sha'ara bi) + noun: يشعر بالتعب (feels tired), تشعر بالسعادة (feels happy). The **بـ** (bi, by/with) introduces the feeling.

sentences.base_language: In the morning, she took a deep breath in front of the mirror and told herself: "No problem — I am capable."
sentences.target_language: في الصباح، أخذت نفساً عميقاً أمام المرآة وقالت لنفسها: «لا مشكلة — أنا قادرة.»
sentences.romanized: Fi as-sabah, akhadat nafasan 'amiqan amama al-mir'at wa-qalat li-nafsiha: «La mushkila — ana qadira.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**أخذت** = she took — feminine past tense: فعَل → فعَل**ت** (he did → she did). The feminine past suffix **ت** (-at/-t) is added after the verb stem: أخذ → أخذ**ت** (she took), قال → قال**ت** (she said), ذهب → ذهب**ت** (she went). One of the most fundamental Arabic morphological patterns.
**لنفسها** = to herself — لـ (li, to/for) + نفس (self) + ها (her): لِنَفسِ**ها**. **نفس** (nafs) = self/soul: قال لنفسه (told himself), by himself = بنفسه. **قادرة** = capable (fem.) — قادر (masc.) + ة (fem. marker). Feminine adjectives must agree with feminine nouns and subjects.

sentences.base_language: She had eggs and bread for breakfast, and drank a cup of milk.
sentences.target_language: تناولت بيضاً وخبزاً على الإفطار وشربت كوباً من الحليب.
sentences.romanized: Tanawwalat baydan wa-khubzan 'ala al-iftar wa-sharabat kawban mina al-halib.
sentences.grammarNote:
**تناول** (tanawwala) = to eat/have (food or medicine) — Form VI (تَفاعَل), often conveying a mutual or deliberate action: تناوَل (took/had/ate). More polished than **أكل** (akala, to eat). In formal contexts: تناول الطعام (had the meal), تناول الدواء (took the medicine).
**كوباً من الحليب** = a cup of milk — the measure phrase: **كوب** (kūb, cup) + **من** (min, of) + الحليب (milk). Arabic expresses 'a cup of X' as كوب **مِن** الحليب. Compare: **زجاجة من الماء** (a bottle of water), **طبق من الأرز** (a plate of rice). The noun of measure takes accusative: كوب**اً**.

sentences.base_language: The office was in the city centre, and she went by metro.
sentences.target_language: كان المكتب في وسط المدينة وذهبت بالمترو.
sentences.romanized: Kana al-maktab fi wasat al-madina wa-dhahabat bil-metro.
literal: in the centre of the city
sentences.grammarNote:
**وسط المدينة** = city centre — إضافة: وسط (centre/middle of) + المدينة (the city). **وسط** (wasat) = middle/centre — used in many contexts: وسط البيت (the middle of the house), في وسط الليل (in the middle of the night).
**بالمترو** = by metro — **بـ** (bi) expresses means of transport: **بـ**السيارة (by car), **بـ**الطائرة (by plane), **بـ**القطار (by train). The **بـ** contracts with the definite article ال: بـ + ال → **بالـ**: بالمترو, بالسيارة. This **بـ** of means is one of the most frequent prepositions in Arabic.

sentences.base_language: On the metro, her hands were sweating from nerves.
sentences.target_language: في المترو، كانت يداها تتعرَّقان من التوتر.
sentences.romanized: Fi al-metro, kanat yadaha tata'arraqan mina at-tawattur.
sentences.grammarNote:
**يداها** = her two hands — **يد** (yad, hand) in the dual: يد**ان** (two hands) + ها (her) → يد**اها** (her two hands). The dual is used because humans have exactly two hands. Arabic consistently uses the dual for naturally occurring pairs: عيناه (his two eyes), أذناها (her two ears), رجلاه (his two feet).
**تتعرَّقان** = they two are sweating — Form V present tense dual feminine: تتعرَّق**ان** (-an marks dual present). **كانت... تتعرَّقان** = were sweating — **كان** + present tense creates the past progressive: كانت **تعمل** (she was working), كان **يأكل** (he was eating). An essential construction for ongoing past actions.

sentences.base_language: She arrived at the company building and looked at her watch: nine-twenty.
sentences.target_language: وصلت إلى مبنى الشركة ونظرت إلى ساعتها: التاسعة والعشرون.
sentences.romanized: Wasalat ila mabna ash-sharika wa-nazarat ila sa'atiha: at-tasi'a wal-'ishrun.
sentences.grammarNote:
**وصل إلى** = arrived at — **إلى** marks destination with **وصل**: وصل **إلى** المدينة (arrived at the city), وصل **إلى** المكتب (arrived at the office). Compare **جاء** (came, also with إلى or without): جاء **إلى** البيت (came home).
**التاسعة والعشرون** = nine twenty, lit. 'the ninth and the twenty' — Arabic time: الساعة **التاسعة** (9:00) + و (and) + **عشرون** (twenty, minutes are stated as a number). Full form: الساعة التاسعة وعشرون دقيقة (the ninth hour and twenty minutes). For half past: **الساعة التاسعة والنصف** (9:30, 'and the half').

sentences.base_language: The receptionist said: "Hello, how can I help you?"
sentences.target_language: قالت موظَّفة الاستقبال: «مرحباً، كيف يمكنني مساعدتك؟»
sentences.romanized: Qalat muwazzafat al-istiqbal: «Marhaban, kayfa yumkinuni musa'adatuk?»
sentences.grammarNote:
**يمكنني** = can I / it is possible for me — **يمكن** (yukmin, it is possible) + ني (me): يمكن**ني** أن أساعدك (I can help you, lit. 'it is possible for me to help you'). **يمكن** is an impersonal verb taking the person as an indirect object: يمكن**ك** (you can), يمكن**ه** (he can), يمكن**نا** (we can).
**مساعدتك** = helping you — مصدر (verbal noun) مساعدة (helping) + ك (you): مساعدت**ك** (helping you). This is a common compressed Arabic construction — using a verbal noun + possessive suffix instead of a full verb clause. **موظَّفة الاستقبال** = the receptionist (fem.) — موظَّف (employee, Form II passive participle of وظَّف) + ة.

sentences.base_language: Sara replied: "Good morning. My name is Sara al-Najjar and I have an interview at nine-thirty."
sentences.target_language: ردَّت سارة: «صباح الخير. اسمي سارة النجار ولديَّ مقابلة الساعة التاسعة والنصف.»
sentences.romanized: Raddat Sara: «Sabah al-khayr. Ismi Sara an-Najjar wa-ladayya muqabala as-sa'a at-tasi'a wan-nisf.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**صباح الخير** = good morning, lit. 'morning of goodness' — an إضافة: صباح (morning of) + الخير (the good). The response is **صباح النور** (morning of light). Arabic greetings often come in pairs: **السلام عليكم** → **وعليكم السلام**, **مرحباً** → **أهلاً**, **صباح الخير** → **صباح النور**.
**اسمي** = my name — اسم (ism, name) + ي (-i, my): اسم**ي** سارة (my name is Sara). The first-person possessive suffix is ـي (-i): بيت**ي** (my house), كتاب**ي** (my book). **لديَّ** = I have — لدى + ي → لديَّ (the ي is doubled with shadda when the suffix ي follows an alif): لديَّ وقت (I have time).

sentences.base_language: The manager, Mr. Husain, arrived, shook her hand, and said: "Nice to meet you."
sentences.target_language: وصل المدير الأستاذ حسين وصافحها وقال: «يسعدني لقاؤك.»
sentences.romanized: Wasala al-mudir al-ustadh Husayn wa-safahahahu wa-qala: «Yas'aduni liqa'uk.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**صافحها** = shook her hand — **صافح** (safaha) = to shake hands with (Form III verb: فاعَل, often reciprocal action): صافح**ها** = shook hands with her (verb + object suffix). Form III verbs often express doing something **with** someone: عامَل (dealt with), قابَل (met/faced), ساعَد (helped).
**يسعدني لقاؤك** = nice to meet you, lit. 'your meeting makes me happy' — **يسعدني** (yas'aduni, makes me happy) + **لقاؤ** (meeting of) + **ك** (you). **لقاء** is the مصدر of لقي (met). A formal, elegant greeting. More casual: أهلاً وسهلاً (welcome), or يسعدني (it makes me happy / pleased to meet you).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Husain asked Sara: "Why do you want to become an engineer?"
sentences.target_language: سأل الأستاذ حسين سارة: «لماذا تريدين أن تصبحي مهندسة؟»
sentences.romanized: Sa'ala al-ustadh Husayn Sara: «Limadha turidina an tusbahi muhandisa?»
sentences.grammarNote:
**تريدين** = you want (feminine you) — Arabic second-person verbs have gender: تريد (you want, masc.) → تريد**ين** (you want, fem.). This feminine marker **ين** (-in) appears in the present tense: تعمل**ين** (you work, fem.), تذهب**ين** (you go, fem.). The feminine address is essential when speaking to women.
**أن تصبحي** = to become (fem.) — **أن** (an, that/to) introduces a subjunctive clause: تريد **أن** تعمل (wants to work), يجب **أن** تدرس (must study). **أن** + present tense verb = infinitive purpose in Arabic. **أصبح** (asabaha) = became/became + adjective: أصبحت مهندسة (became an engineer).

sentences.base_language: Sara replied: "Because I love designing and building software to help people."
sentences.target_language: أجابت سارة: «لأنني أحبّ تصميم وبناء البرمجيات لمساعدة الناس.»
sentences.romanized: Ajabat Sara: «Li-annani uhibbu tasmim wa-bina' al-barmajiyyat li-musa'adat an-nas.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**أجابت** = she answered — Form IV of جاب (أَجاب): أجاب**ت** (she answered). Form IV (أَفعَل) is often causative: أخبر (informed), أرسل (sent), أجاب (responded).
**تصميم** and **بناء** = designing and building — مصادر (verbal nouns): تصميم is the مصدر of صمَّم (designed, Form II), and بناء is the مصدر of بنى (built, Form I). Arabic uses مصادر as objects of the verb: أحبّ **تصميم** البرامج (I love designing programs). **لمساعدة** = in order to help — **لـ** + مصدر expresses purpose: لِـ**مساعدة** (to help), لِـ**دراسة** (to study).

sentences.base_language: Mr. Husain nodded, smiled, and said: "Very good answers." He seemed very satisfied.
sentences.target_language: أومأ الأستاذ حسين وابتسم وقال: «إجاباتٌ جيدة جداً.» بدا راضياً جداً.
sentences.romanized: Awma'a al-ustadh Husayn wa-ibtasama wa-qala: «Ijabatun jayyida jiddan.» Bada radian jiddan.
sentences.grammarNote:
**أومأ** = nodded — the verb for nodding one's head: أومأ برأسه (nodded with his head). **ابتسم** = smiled — Form VIII (افتَعَل) of بَسَم: ابتَسَمَ. Form VIII verbs often have a reflexive or achieved meaning: اجتمع (gathered/met), اهتمّ (paid attention), ابتسم (smiled).
**بدا** = seemed/appeared — Form I of بدا: يبدو (seems). بدا **راضياً** (seemed satisfied) — the predicate راضياً is in the accusative (كان/بدا + predicate). **راضٍ** (radin) = content/satisfied — this is a defective adjective (ending in ي in the indefinite nominative) which becomes **راضياً** in the accusative: a Form I active participle of رضي (to be content/satisfied).

sentences.base_language: After the interview, Sara felt much calmer.
sentences.target_language: بعد المقابلة، شعرت سارة بهدوء أكبر بكثير.
sentences.romanized: Ba'da al-muqabala, sha'arat Sara bi-hudo' akbar bi-kathir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**شعرت** = she felt — past feminine: شعر → شعر**ت**. **شعر بـ** = to feel: شعر **بالسعادة** (felt happiness), شعر **بالبرد** (felt cold). **بـ** introduces the experienced feeling or sensation.
**أكبر بكثير** = much more (bigger by a lot) — the comparative: **أفعل** pattern (أكبر from كبير, أحسن from حسن, أجمل from جميل). **بكثير** (bi-kathir, by a lot) intensifies a comparative: أكبر **بكثير** (much bigger), أحسن **بكثير** (much better). This **بـ** of degree is the standard comparative intensifier in Arabic — like English 'by far' or 'much.'

sentences.base_language: She left the building and found a café nearby.
sentences.target_language: غادرت المبنى ووجدت مقهىً قريباً.
sentences.romanized: Ghadarat al-mabna wa-wajdat maqhan qariban.
sentences.grammarNote:
**غادر** = to leave/depart — Form III verb (فاعَل): غادر**ت** المبنى (left the building). **غادر** is more formal than **خرج من** (went out of). Used for departing a place deliberately: غادر البلاد (left the country), غادر الفندق (checked out of the hotel).
**مقهىً** — the indefinite accusative of مقهى (café, an alif maqsura noun): مقهى → مقهىً (with tanwin on the alif: ً). Nouns ending in alif maqsura (ى) are called **مقصور**: they carry tanwin differently from regular nouns. **وجد** (wajada) = found — Form I: وجد → وجدت (she found).

sentences.base_language: She ordered a coffee, sat down, and sent her mother a message.
sentences.target_language: طلبت قهوةً وجلست وأرسلت رسالةً إلى والدتها.
sentences.romanized: Talabat qahwatan wa-jalassat wa-arsalat risalatan ila walidatiha.
sentences.grammarNote:
**والدتها** = her mother — والدة (mother) + ها (her) → والدت**ها**. **والد** (father) and **والدة** (mother) are the formal terms; **أبي** (my father) and **أمّي** (my mother) are the everyday forms. Note the ة becomes ت before the suffix: والد**ة** → والد**ت**ها.
**طلبت** = she ordered — **طلب** (talaba, to request/order): طلب الطعام (ordered food), طلب المساعدة (asked for help). **أرسلت رسالة إلى** = sent a message to — **أرسل** (arsala, Form IV: to send) + direct object (رسالة) + **إلى** (to, marking the recipient). The same structure as in other languages: send [thing] to [person].

sentences.base_language: She wrote: "Mom, the interview is over! I think it went well."
sentences.target_language: كتبت: «أمّي، انتهت المقابلة! أظنّ أنها سارت بشكل جيد.»
sentences.romanized: Katabat: «Ummi, intaghat al-muqabala! Azunnu annaha sarat bi-shakl jayyid.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**انتهت** = it ended (fem.) — **انتهى** is Form VIII (افتَعَل) of نهى: انتهى (ended, intransitive). The verb agrees in gender with its subject المقابلة (the interview, fem.): انته**ت** (not انتهى). In Arabic, verbs must agree with their subject in gender (and number when the subject precedes the verb).
**بشكل جيد** = in a good manner — **بشكل** (bi-shakl, in a manner/shape) + adjective: بشكل **ممتاز** (in an excellent manner), بشكل **صحيح** (correctly). A very common adverbial construction in Modern Arabic — often used where English would use an adverb. **أظنّ أنّ** = I think that — **ظنَّ** (zanna, to think/suppose) + **أنَّ** + clause.

sentences.base_language: Her mother replied quickly: "How wonderful! Tonight I'll prepare the fish you love most."
sentences.target_language: ردَّت والدتها بسرعة: «ما أحسن ذلك! الليلة سأعدّ لكِ السمك الذي تحبّينه أكثر.»
sentences.romanized: Raddat walidatuha bi-sur'a: «Ma ahsana dhalika! Al-layla sa-u'iddu laki as-samak alladhi tuhibbinathu akthar.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**ما أحسن ذلك!** = how wonderful that is! — **ما أَفعَل** is the Arabic exclamation of admiration (**تعجّب**, ta'ajjub): ما أجمل**ها**! (how beautiful she is!), ما أحسن**ه**! (how good it is!). The pattern is: **ما** + **أَفعَل** + the subject (sometimes as suffix). An elegant classical construction.
**الذي تحبّينه** = that you (fem.) love — **الذي** (alladhi) is the masculine relative pronoun: الكتاب **الذي** (the book that/which). The relative pronoun must agree in gender and number with its antecedent: الكتاب (masc.) → **الذي**, المدرسة (fem.) → **التي**, الكتب (masc. pl.) → **الذين/التي**. The suffix ه on تحبّينه refers back to السمك.

sentences.base_language: Sara smiled, feeling very happy. She leisurely drank her coffee and looked at the street through the window.
sentences.target_language: ابتسمت سارة وهي تشعر بسعادة بالغة. شربت قهوتها على مهل ونظرت إلى الشارع من النافذة.
sentences.romanized: Ibtasamat Sara wa-hiya tash'ur bi-sa'ada baligha. Sharabat qahwataha 'ala mahl wa-nazarat ila ash-shari' mina an-nafidha.
sentences.grammarNote:
**وهي تشعر** = while she feels — **و** (wa, and) + **هي** (hiya, she) + present verb = a حال clause (circumstantial clause describing how the subject was while the main action happened): ذهب **وهو يغني** (went while singing), جلست **وهي تبكي** (sat while crying). This construction is extremely common in Arabic narrative.
**على مهل** = at leisure / unhurriedly — a fixed expression: تصرَّف **على مهل** (acted unhurriedly), تكلَّم **على مهل** (spoke slowly/leisurely). **قهوتها** = her coffee — قهوة + ها → قهوت**ها** (the ة becomes ت before suffixes: قهوة → قهوت**ه/ها**). **من النافذة** = from/through the window.

sentences.base_language: Whatever the result, she knew that she had already given her best effort.
sentences.target_language: مهما كانت النتيجة، كانت تعلم أنها أعطت قصارى جهدها.
sentences.romanized: Mahma kanat an-natija, kanat ta'lam annaha a'tat qusara juhdiya.
sentences.grammarNote:
**مهما** = whatever — a conditional particle: **مهما** كانت النتيجة (whatever the result was). **مهما** introduces conditions of indifference: **مهما** قلتَ (whatever you say), **مهما** حدث (whatever happens). A counterpart to **كيفما** (however) and **حيثما** (wherever).
**قصارى جهدها** = the utmost of her effort — **قصارى** is a rare word meaning 'the utmost/maximum': قصارى الجهد (the utmost effort), قصارى ما يستطيع (the best he can). Used in the fixed expression: **بذل قصارى جهده** (did his utmost). **جهد** (juhd) = effort/exertion + **ها** (her) → جهد**ها** (her effort). **أعطت** = she gave — past fem. of أعطى (Form IV of عطا): أعطى → أعطـ**ت**.
